summary: Gruber responds to Dave Winer's claim that Apple's boycott of Flash 'isn't working' by arguing it's too early to declare a winner. He frames the battle with clear win/loss conditions for each side: Adobe loses if publishers abandon Flash, Apple loses if missing Flash drives customers to competing devices. He notes that while many major sites now offer iOS-compatible alternatives alongside Flash (not instead of it), Apple's device sales remain the true barometer of who's winning. With Android 2.2 only just arriving and no Flash-capable iPad competitors yet, the verdict is still out.
key_points: The battle should be judged by concrete metrics: for Adobe, whether publishers abandon Flash; for Apple, whether missing Flash hurts device sales
Major websites are adding iOS-compatible alternatives alongside Flash rather than replacing it — partial progress for Apple, not yet a disaster for Adobe
iOS has become the leading driver of H.264 HTML5 video adoption on the web
Sales volume is the real pressure gauge — the more iOS devices sold, the more leverage Apple has and the more incentive publishers have to drop Flash-only content
Flash's decline, if it comes, will be long and slow because no single alternative has comparable market share
money_quotes: He's right that Apple hasn't won this battle, but I think he's wrong that they're losing. It's still far too early to tell.

For Apple, losing would be if the absence of Flash on iOS devices led to people choosing competing devices that do support Flash — i.e. if the absence of Flash for iOS hurt sales of iPhones, iPod Touches, and iPads.
From the perspective of iPhone users, though, there's a lot less 'missing content' now than there was in 2007.
Before the iPhone, Flash's advantage was that it was installed on every major web-browsing computer by default. Now, there is a major web browsing OS where Flash is not supported at all.
If Adobe loses this battle, Flash's decline is going to be long and slow.
Such pressure — both on Apple and website publishers — is best measured not by complaints on the web, but by sales of iPhones, iPods, and iPads.
The more they sell, the less likely Apple is to cave on Flash support, and the more likely it is for websites to stop publishing Flash-only content.
key_insight: The Apple-Flash battle should be judged not by how much Flash content still exists, but by whether its absence actually costs Apple device sales — and so far, it hasn't.
